theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.S₃_eq_zero_of_chord`, `secp256k1_semaev_three_chord
theorems.claim: Semaev's 3rd summation polynomial — forward direction (`S₃_eq_zero_of_chord`: for `y = x³ + a·x + b` over a field, if `(x₁,y₁), (x₂,y₂)` are curve points with `x₁ ≠ x₂` and `x₃` is the `x`-coordinate of their chord-sum, then `S₃(x₁,x₂,x₃) = 0` — equivalently `P₁+P₂+P₃ = O ⇒ S₃ = 0`; `secp256k1_semaev_three_chord` specializes it to secp256k1 `y = x³ + 7`. First Semaev summation polynomial formalized in Lean/Mathlib (a green-field `BARRIERS.md` item). Certificate designed + verified by exact symbolic elimination (sympy: Gröbner + iterated resultant), kernel-checked via `linear_combination`. A construction, not an attack — a necessary collinearity condition that by itself computes nothing about any discrete log)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.S₃_eq_zero_of_tangent`, `secp256k1_semaev_three_tangent
theorems.claim: Semaev's `S₃` — tangent / doubling case (`S₃_eq_zero_of_tangent`: the `x₁ = x₂` companion of the chord case — if `(x₁,y₁)` is a curve point and `x₃` is the `x`-coordinate of `2·(x₁,y₁)` (cleared doubling form `4·y₁·(x₃+2·x₁) = (3·x₁+a)`), then `S₃(x₁,x₁,x₃) = 0`; `secp256k1_semaev_three_tangent` specializes to secp256k1. With the chord case this completes the `S₃` forward direction in every nondegenerate case. No `y₁·y₂` cross term arises, so a single certified `linear_combination` suffices)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.secp256k1_semaev_three_point
theorems.claim: Semaev's `S₃` on actual secp256k1 curve points (`secp256k1_semaev_three_point`: for genuine points of the Mathlib elliptic curve `secp256k1.toAffine.Point`, the group relation `P₁+P₂+P₃ = O` with `x(P₁) ≠ x(P₂)` implies `S₃(x₁,x₂,x₃) = 0`. Connects the abstract chord identity to Mathlib's formalized elliptic-curve group law by unfolding `Point.add_of_X_ne` / `slope` / `addX` to recover `x₃ = x(P₁+P₂)` — the honest statement whose hypothesis is the actual curve addition, not a raw coordinate equation)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.secp256k1_semaev_three_point_double
theorems.claim: Semaev's `S₃` on actual secp256k1 points — tangent case (`secp256k1_semaev_three_point_double`: the `P₁ = P₂` companion — for genuine points of `secp256k1.toAffine.Point`, `2•P₁ + P₃ = O` with `P₁` not 2-torsion (`y₁ ≠ negY x₁ y₁`) implies `S₃(x₁,x₁,x₃) = 0`, via Mathlib's tangent doubling `Point.add_of_Y_ne`. With the chord point theorem, the `S₃` forward direction now holds on Mathlib's actual elliptic-curve group law in every nondegenerate case)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.secp256k1_point_decomposition_semaev
theorems.claim: Point decomposition ⇒ Semaev relation (index-calculus entry point) (`secp256k1_point_decomposition_semaev`: if a curve point `R` decomposes as a sum `R = P₁ + P₂` with `x(P₁) ≠ x(P₂)`, then `S₃(x(P₁), x(P₂), x(R)) = 0` — every 2-term decomposition of `R` lies on the Semaev variety. This is the structural reduction index calculus rests on: the search for decompositions is confined to the roots of a single codimension-1 polynomial condition, not the whole group. Proof recasts `R = P₁+P₂` as `P₁+P₂+(−R)=0` and uses `x(−R) = x(R)` (`Point.neg_some`) to hand off to `secp256k1_semaev_three_point`. Forward/structural half only — records *where* solutions live, not the cost of *finding* them; over `𝔽_p` that root-finding is the studied `Θ(√n)` barrier)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.secp256k1_point_decomposition_semaev_double
theorems.claim: Point decomposition ⇒ Semaev relation — doubling case (`secp256k1_point_decomposition_semaev_double`: the `P₁ = P₂` companion — if `R = 2·P₁` with `P₁` not 2-torsion, then `S₃(x(P₁), x(P₁), x(R)) = 0`. With the generic-sum theorem, covers every 2-term decomposition of `R`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.secp256k1_S₃poly_natDegree
theorems.claim: Semaev polynomial has degree exactly 2 in each variable (`secp256k1_S₃poly_natDegree`: for `x₁ ≠ x₂` over `𝔽_p`, `S₃(x₁,x₂,·)` is a degree-exactly-2 univariate polynomial, leading coefficient `(x₁−x₂) ≠ 0`. Base case of the classical `deg_{xᵢ} Sₘ = 2^{m−2}` degree tower — the doubling that makes higher-order decompositions blow up. Via `Polynomial.natDegree_quadratic`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.secp256k1_decomposition_completions_le_two
theorems.claim: Bounded decomposition fan-out (index-calculus cost ingredient) (`secp256k1_decomposition_completions_le_two`: fixing a base coordinate `x₁` and the target `x_R` with `x₁ ≠ x_R`, at most 2 field elements `x₂` complete a 2-decomposition `S₃(x₁,x₂,x_R)=0` — any finite set of completions has card `≤ 2`. A degree-2 polynomial over a field has `≤ 2` roots, so the index-calculus relation graph has bounded fan-out at each factor-base point. The one exact, kernel-checked quantitative fact under the prime-field cost analysis — *not* the hardness claim itself, which stays an open conjecture; see `BARRIERS.md`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.S₃_root_of_eq_zero
theorems.claim: Semaev's `S₃` — reverse direction (the two roots) (`S₃_root_of_eq_zero`: `S₃` is quadratic in `x₃` with leading coefficient `(x₁−x₂)`; if `(x₁,y₁), (x₂,y₂)` lie on `y = x³ + a·x + b` and `S₃(x₁,x₂,x₃) = 0`, then `(x₁−x₂)·x₃` equals `(y₂−y₁) − (x₁+x₂)(x₁−x₂)` or `(y₂+y₁) − (x₁+x₂)(x₁−x₂)` — the cleared `x`-coordinates of `P₁+P₂` and `P₁−P₂`. Via a sympy-certified master factorization `(x₁−x₂)·S₃ = (D·x₃−R₊)(D·x₃−R₋)`, a pure `ring` identity, then `mul_eq_zero`; needs no `x₁ ≠ x₂`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.S₃_eq_zero_iff`, `secp256k1_semaev_three_iff
theorems.claim: Semaev's `S₃` — full characterization (iff) (`S₃_eq_zero_iff`: for on-curve `(x₁,y₁), (x₂,y₂)` with `x₁ ≠ x₂`, `S₃(x₁,x₂,x₃) = 0 ⟺ (x₁−x₂)(x₁+x₂+x₃) = (y₂−y₁)  ∨  = (y₂+y₁)` — i.e. `x₃` is the `x`-coordinate of `P₁+P₂` or of `P₁−P₂`; `secp256k1_semaev_three_iff` specializes to secp256k1. Combines forward + reverse: the complete statement of `S₃` as the elimination of `y₁,y₂` from "three points sum to `O`")

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.S₄`, `S₄_eq_zero_of_common_root`, `secp256k1_semaev_four_of_common_root
theorems.claim: Semaev's 4th summation polynomial `S₄` — definition + forward direction (`S₄ = Res_X(S₃(x₁,x₂,X), S₃(x₃,x₄,X))` via Mathlib's `Polynomial.resultant _ _ 2 2`; `S₄_eq_zero_of_common_root`: a shared root `X₀` of the two `S₃` slices — e.g. `X₀ = x(P₁+P₂) = x(P₃+P₄)` — forces `S₄ = 0`, over any commutative ring, via the resultant Bézout identity `exists_mul_add_mul_eq_C_resultant`. `secp256k1_semaev_four_of_common_root` specializes to secp256k1. The first rung above `S₃` — the recursion index calculus over `𝔽_{p^k}` uses. First `S₄` in Lean)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.resultant_eq_zero_of_common_root
theorems.claim: Semaev forward direction generalized to all `n` — the resultant recursion engine (`resultant_eq_zero_of_common_root`: for *any* two univariate polynomials `f, g` over a commutative ring sharing an evaluation-root `X₀` (`f(X₀) = g(X₀) = 0`), `Res(f,g,m,n) = 0`, provided the Sylvester sizes are not both zero. Every Semaev step `Sₙ₊₁ = Res_X(Sₙ(…,X), S₃(…,X))` inherits its forward direction from this one lemma at level `n`; `S₄_eq_zero_of_common_root` is now literally the instance `f = S₃(x₁,x₂,·)`, `g = S₃(x₃,x₄,·)`, and `S₅, S₆, …` are discharged verbatim with `f` the previous slice. The mechanism-level generalization of the Semaev forward direction to the whole infinite `Sₙ` family — via the Bézout identity `exists_mul_add_mul_eq_C_resultant`, over any commutative ring, no field or closure)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.S₃poly_eval`, `S₄_block_swap`, `S₄_symm₁₂`, `S₄_symm₃₄
theorems.claim: *(alternate/supporting)* `S₄` infrastructure — the `S₃`-as-polynomial bridge `S₃poly_eval` (`(S₃poly …).eval x₃ = S₃ …`) and `S₄`'s full symmetry: block swap `S₄_block_swap` `(x₁,x₂)↔(x₃,x₄)` (from `resultant_comm`) and within-pair `S₄_symm₁₂`, `S₄_symm₃₄` — so `S₄` is symmetric on all of `{x₁,x₂,x₃,x₄}`, the symmetry a summation polynomial must have

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.S₄_common_root_of_eq_zero`, `secp256k1_semaev_four_common_root_of_eq_zero
theorems.claim: Semaev's `S₄` — reverse/meaning direction (`S₄_common_root_of_eq_zero`: over a field, for on-curve `(x₁,y₁), (x₂,y₂)` with `x₁ ≠ x₂` and `S₄(x₁,x₂,x₃,x₄) = 0`, the two `S₃` slices share a root `X₀` in the field — the converse of `S₄_eq_zero_of_common_root`. Since `S₄` is the resultant, its vanishing forces a shared root; it lies *in* `K` because `S₃(x₁,x₂,·)` splits with the explicitly known roots `x(P₁±P₂)`. Via `resultant_eq_prod_eval`; `secp256k1_semaev_four_common_root_of_eq_zero` specializes. With the forward direction, `S₄` now holds both ways)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.S₃poly_master_factor
theorems.claim: S₃`-slice two-root factorization over `F[X]` (`S₃poly_master_factor`: `(x₁−x₂)·S₃poly = (D·X − R₊)·(D·X − R₋)` as a polynomial identity in `F[X]` — the lift of the scalar master factorization, exhibiting `S₃`'s degree-2 slice as split with the explicit roots `x(P₁±P₂)`; the key input to the `S₄` reverse)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Semaev.S₃_symm₁₂`, `S₃_symm₂₃
theorems.claim: *(alternate/supporting)* Semaev `S₃` full symmetry — `S₃_symm₁₂` (swap `x₁,x₂`) and `S₃_symm₂₃` (swap `x₂,x₃`), so `S₃` is symmetric in all three `x`-arguments, as a summation polynomial must be

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_double_x_eq_Φ₂_div_Ψ₂Sq`, `secp256k1_Φ₂
theorems.claim: Multiplication-by-2 `x`-coordinate formula in Mathlib's canonical division polynomials (`secp256k1_double_x_eq_Φ₂_div_Ψ₂Sq`: for a non-2-torsion point `P=(x,y)` on `y=x³+7`, the doubled `x`-coordinate `addX x x (slope x x y y) = (Φ 2)(x)/(Ψ₂Sq)(x)` — with Mathlib's own `Φ`/`ΨSq` division polynomials, `Φ 2 = X⁴−56X` (`secp256k1_Φ₂`), `Ψ₂Sq = 4X³+28 = 4y`. This is the base case `n=2` of the general multiplication formula `x([n]P)=Φₙ/ΨSqₙ` — the engine of the general `ψₙ`-vanishing ⟺ `n`-torsion bridge (`notes/FOUNDATIONS.md` rung 4). Unlike the per-`n` bridges (which used ad-hoc reduced polynomials like `3x⁴+84x`), this states the *canonical* `Φₙ/ΨSqₙ` shape. The general-`n` formula, by induction on the division-polynomial recurrence, is the missing Mathlib rung — absent from v4.31, present only in a stalled upstream PR)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_double_y_eq_ω₂
theorems.claim: Multiplication-by-2 `y`-coordinate formula `y(2•P) = ω₂/(2y)³` (N7-uniform S3a base) (`secp256k1_double_y_eq_ω₂`: for a non-2-torsion point `P=(x,y)` on `y=x³+7`, the doubled `y`-coordinate `addY x x y (slope x x y y) = (x⁶ + 140x³ − 392)/(2y)³` — the `y`-analogue of the `n=2` `x`-formula and the first `y`-coordinate division-polynomial value in the repo, i.e. `y(2•P) = ω₂/ψ₂³` with `ψ₂ = 2y` and the `n=2` `y`-coordinate ("omega") polynomial `ω₂ = x⁶ + 140x³ − 392`. Mathlib has no `ω` division polynomial (open `TODO`); the value is derived from the group law's doubling `addY` and closed by one certified `linear_combination` against the curve equation and the slope relation `2y·ℓ = 3x`. Base case of node S3a of the N7-uniform build (`BARRIERS.md §B3`; target `n7_uniform_secp256k1_x`). Honest scope: fixed `n=2` coordinate identity only; the uniform `y([n]P)=ωₙ/ψₙ³` and the general bivariate `ωₙ` remain open. No `native_decide`, no new axioms)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_two_nsmul_coords
theorems.claim: Point-level doubling `2•P = (Φ₂/Ψ₂Sq, ω₂/(2y)³)` on secp256k1's group (N7-uniform S3a) (`secp256k1_two_nsmul_coords`: for a nonsingular non-2-torsion `P=(x,y)` on `y=x³+7`, if the double `2 • Point.some x y h` is the affine point `Point.some X Y h'` then both coordinates are pinned to their division-polynomial values: `X = Φ₂(x)/Ψ₂Sq(x)` and `Y = (x⁶+140x³−392)/(2y)³`. Connects the two landed coordinate identities to the actual group operation `2 • P` on `secp256k1.toAffine.Point` (via `two_nsmul` + `Point.add_self_of_Y_ne` + the doubled point's coordinate injectivity) — the `n=2` Point-level instance of the open target `n7_uniform_secp256k1_x` and the base case of node S3a of the N7-uniform build. Honest scope: fixed `n=2` only; the uniform statement for all `n` remains open. No `native_decide`, no new axioms)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_triple_y_eq_ω₃
theorems.claim: Multiplication-by-3 `y`-coordinate formula `y(3•P) = ω₃/ψ₃³` (N7-uniform S3a) (`secp256k1_triple_y_eq_ω₃`: in the chord-tangent slope parametrisation `s₂` (`2y·s₂=3x`), `s₃` (`(s₂−3x)·s₃ = −(s₂(s₂−3x)+y)−y`) of the `x`-triple, the tripled `y`-coordinate `s₃·(s₂−s₃)−y` equals `ω₃/(3x⁴+84x)³` with the `n=3` `y`-coordinate ("omega") polynomial `ω₃ = y·(x+1540x⁹−87024x⁶−109760x³−1229312)` — bivariate, the `y` factor being the odd-`n` shape. Companion to the `x`-triple `x(3•P)=Φ₃/ΨSq₃` and extends the `n=2` `y`-formula. Mathlib has no `ω` division polynomial (open `TODO`). Proof mirrors the `x`-triple: Step A clears `s₃` via `hℓ3` into an `s₃`-free `W`-expression, Step B is the `s₃`-free master identity cleared by `(2y)³` (CAS-derived `linear_combination` cofactors), then cancel `(s₂−3x)³`. Honest scope: fixed `n=3` coordinate identity; the uniform `y([n]P)=ωₙ/ψₙ³` remains open. No `native_decide`, no new axioms)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_Φ₃`, `secp256k1_ΨSq₃`, `secp256k1_Φ₃_eval`, `secp256k1_ΨSq₃_eval
theorems.claim: n = 3` division polynomials `Φ₃`/`ΨSq₃` for secp256k1 — canonical + explicit forms (`secp256k1_Φ₃`: `Φ 3 = X·Ψ₃ − preΨ₄·Ψ₂Sq`; `secp256k1_ΨSq₃`: `ΨSq 3 = Ψ₃` — Mathlib's canonical `Φ_three`/`ΨSq_three` specialised to secp256k1. Substituting the concrete `Ψ₃ = 3X⁴+84X`, `preΨ₄ = 2X⁶+280X³−784`, `Ψ₂Sq = 4X³+28` gives the explicit degree-9 numerator `Φ₃(x) = x⁹−672x⁶+2352x³+21952` (`secp256k1_Φ₃_eval`) and degree-8 denominator `ΨSq₃(x) = 9x⁸+504x⁵+7056x` (`secp256k1_ΨSq₃_eval`) of `x(3•P) = Φ₃/ΨSq₃`. Groundwork for node N7@3 and the degree half of N10(i) (`deg [n] = n`): the numerator degree `9 = 3` is the general `deg [n] = n` prediction at `n = 3`. Ring identities in `𝔽_p[X]`, no `native_decide`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_triple_x_eq_Φ₃_div_ΨSq₃
theorems.claim: Multiplication-by-3 `x`-coordinate formula for secp256k1 — `x(3•P) = Φ₃/ΨSq₃` (`secp256k1_triple_x_eq_Φ₃_div_ΨSq₃`: for `P=(x,y)` on `y=x³+7` with `y≠0`, `Ψ₃(x)≠0`, and the chord-tangent slopes `s₂` (`2y·s₂=3x`) / `s₃` (secant `2P→P`, the `FiveTorsionBridge` parametrisation), the tripled `x`-coordinate `s₃−(s₂−2x)−x = (Φ 3)(x)/(ΨSq 3)(x)` in Mathlib's canonical division polynomials. Node N7@3 — extends the `n=2` case `secp256k1_double_x_eq_Φ₂_div_Ψ₂Sq`, connecting the concrete `Φ₃`/`ΨSq₃` forms to the actual elliptic-curve group law. Proof mirrors `five_core`: square the secant relation to clear `s₃`, then a CAS-derived `linear_combination` of the slope + curve equations after clearing `(2y)⁴`; certificate `scripts/certs/triple_mult_formula_check.py` (`CERT_OK`). No `native_decide`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.secp256k1_torsion_iff_principal
theorems.claim: Weil-pairing foundations, rung 1 — torsion ⟺ principal divisor (`secp256k1_torsion_iff_principal`: a point `P` of secp256k1 is `n`-torsion `⟺ n • toClass P = 0`, i.e. iff the divisor `n·([P] − [O])` is principal in the coordinate ring `F[secp256k1]`. Obtained by transporting `n`-torsion across Mathlib's Abel–Jacobi map `toClass : W.Point →+ Additive (ClassGroup W.CoordinateRing)` — the injective embedding `E ↪ Pic` on which Mathlib *builds* the group law — via `toClass_eq_zero` + `map_nsmul`. This is the first genuine rung of the Weil pairing construction: principality of `n·([P] − [O])` is the existence precondition for the Miller function `f_P`. Key finding: the divisor-class substrate (`toClass`, `FunctionField`, `rootsOfUnity`) is already in Mathlib, so the pairing is more reachable than "from zero" — remaining rungs (extract `f_P`, evaluate at divisors, Weil reciprocity, bilinear `eₙ→μₙ`) are the open frontier, `notes/FOUNDATIONS.md`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.secp256k1_miller_function_exists
theorems.claim: Weil-pairing foundations, rung 2 — the Miller function exists (`secp256k1_miller_function_exists`: for a nonzero `n`-torsion point `P=(x,y)` of secp256k1, there exists `f_P` in the function field `F(secp256k1)` generating the principal fractional ideal `(⟨X−x, Y−y⟩)ⁿ = (XYIdeal' h)ⁿ` — the Miller function, whose divisor is `n·([P] − [O])`. Extracted from rung 1: `n`-torsion trivializes the class `ClassGroup.mk ((XYIdeal' h)ⁿ) = (mk (XYIdeal' h))ⁿ = 1`, and Mathlib's `ClassGroup.mk_eq_one_iff` turns a trivial class into a principal ideal, whose generator is `f_P`. Rung W2 of the Weil sub-ladder — the input to Weil reciprocity / the pairing `eₙ`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.secp256k1_miller_function_unique
theorems.claim: Weil-pairing foundations, rung 3 — the Miller function is unique up to a unit (`secp256k1_miller_function_unique`: two generators `f, g` of the same principal ideal `(XYIdeal' h)ⁿ` differ by a unit of the coordinate ring, `∃ u : F[secp256k1]ˣ, u • f = g` — the well-definedness input for the Weil pairing `eₙ` (its value must not depend on the chosen Miller function). Representative-independence half of rung W3. Proof designed by the Fable model and kernel-verified — a piloted "strong-model + Lean-kernel" loop: Fable read the Mathlib source, identified `Submodule.span_singleton_eq_span_singleton`, and produced the proof, which the kernel accepted)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.evalAt`, `evalAt_surjective`, `evalAt_ker
theorems.claim: Weil-pairing infrastructure — point-evaluation homomorphism (`evalAt`: for a rational point `(x,y)` on any Weierstrass curve over a field, evaluation of a regular function at `P` as a ring hom `F[E] →+* F`, built from Mathlib's `quotientXYIdealEquiv` (`F[E]/⟨X−x,Y−y⟩ ≃ F`); `evalAt_surjective` (every value attained — `P` is `F`-rational) and `evalAt_ker` (kernel `= ⟨X−x, Y−y⟩`, the maximal ideal of `P` — the functions vanishing at `P`). The first rung of the function-evaluation layer Mathlib lacks (v4.31 has no rational-function evaluation API and `F[E]` is not known to be Dedekind there); regular-function evaluation now exists, the rational extension via localization is next. Unblocks the Weil pairing's `f_P(D_Q)` step)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.xyIdeal_isMaximal
theorems.claim: A rational point is a closed point (maximal ideal) (`xyIdeal_isMaximal`: for `(x,y)` on any Weierstrass curve over a field, the ideal `⟨X−x, Y−y⟩` of `F[E]` is maximal — since the quotient `F[E]/⟨X−x,Y−y⟩ ≃ F` is a field. Establishes each `F`-rational point as a closed point of the affine curve; the prerequisite for localizing at `P` (the local ring `F[E]_P`) to evaluate rational functions regular at `P` — the next infrastructure rung toward the Weil pairing's `f_P(D_Q)`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.evalRatAt_algebraMap
theorems.claim: evalRatAt` extends `evalAt` (`evalRatAt_algebraMap`: for `(x,y)` on any Weierstrass curve over a field and a regular function `r ∈ F[E]`, the value of its image in the local ring `F[E]_P = Localization.AtPrime ⟨X−x,Y−y⟩` under `evalRatAt` equals its direct value `evalAt h r`. The correctness certificate that rational-function evaluation genuinely extends regular-function evaluation — the residue of the localization at `P` coincides *definitionally* with the quotient by `P`'s maximal ideal. Closes the evaluation layer before the Miller function `f_P` is evaluated; proof developed on the warm-server `lake env lean` loop)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.xyIdeal_ne_of_x_ne
theorems.claim: Distinct x-coordinates ⇒ distinct closed points (`xyIdeal_ne_of_x_ne`: rational points on a Weierstrass curve with `x₁ ≠ x₂` give different maximal ideals `⟨X−x, Y−y⟩` of `F[E]`. Proof: `XClass x₁ − XClass x₂ = algebraMap F F[E] (x₂−x₁)` is a unit when `x₁ ≠ x₂`; equal ideals would force that unit into a maximal (proper) ideal — contradiction. The divisor-support separation the Weil pairing needs — the points of `D_Q` chosen to compute `f_P(D_Q)` must avoid `P`, `O`, which requires distinct points to be genuinely distinct closed points; scouted + proved on the warm-server loop)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.xyIdeal_ne_of_ne` (+ `xyIdeal_ne_of_y_ne`)
theorems.claim: Distinct points ⇒ distinct closed points (full separation) (`xyIdeal_ne_of_ne`: for rational points `(x₁,y₁) ≠ (x₂,y₂)` on a Weierstrass curve the maximal ideals `⟨X−x, Y−y⟩` differ — combining the x-coordinate case with the `YClass` companion `xyIdeal_ne_of_y_ne` (`y₁ ≠ y₂ ⇒` distinct ideals, same unit-in-a-maximal-ideal argument on `Y − ·`). The complete divisor-support-separation lemma for the Weil pairing: any two distinct rational points are distinct closed points of `F[E]`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.evalRatAt_eq_zero_iff
theorems.claim: Weil W3 — zero locus of point-evaluation (`evalRatAt_eq_zero_iff`: a rational function regular at `P` vanishes at `P` iff it lies in the maximal ideal of the local ring `F[E]_P`. From `evalRatAt = residueFieldEquiv ∘ residue`: residue-field iso injective, so `evalRatAt r = 0 ↔ residue r = 0 ↔ r ∈ 𝔪`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.evalRatAt_ne_zero_iff_isUnit
theorems.claim: Weil W3 — nonvanishing criterion at a point (`evalRatAt_ne_zero_iff_isUnit`: a rational function regular at `P` is nonzero at `P` iff it is a unit of the local ring `F[E]_P` — exactly the condition to evaluate a Miller function `f_P` to a nonzero value in `𝔽_p`, off its zero/pole support. Completes the *point-evaluation* half of Weil rung W3; `f_P(D_Q)` over a whole divisor and Weil reciprocity (W4) remain genuine Mathlib gaps — see `BARRIERS.md`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.secp256k1_psi3_root_iff_class_torsion
theorems.claim: ψ₃ root ⟺ Miller divisor `3·([P]−[O])` principal (`secp256k1_psi3_root_iff_class_torsion`: for a nonzero affine `P=(x,y)` of secp256k1, `(ψ 3).evalEval x y = 0 ↔ 3 • toClass P = 0` — the 3-division polynomial vanishes at `P` iff the Abel–Jacobi class is 3-torsion, i.e. iff `3·([P]−[O])` is principal. Welds the division-polynomial torsion tower to Weil divisor-class rung 1 at their shared middle `3•P=0`; the first result letting a computable coordinate condition `ψ 3 = 0` *decide* the divisor-theoretic principality the Miller function needs)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.secp256k1_psi5_root_iff_class_torsion
theorems.claim: ψ₅ root ⟺ Miller divisor `5·([P]−[O])` principal (`secp256k1_psi5_root_iff_class_torsion`: the `n=5` analogue — `(ψ 5).evalEval x y = 0 ↔ 5 • toClass P = 0`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.secp256k1_psi7_root_iff_class_torsion
theorems.claim: ψ₇ root ⟺ Miller divisor `7·([P]−[O])` principal (`secp256k1_psi7_root_iff_class_torsion`: the `n=7` analogue — `(ψ 7).evalEval x y = 0 ↔ 7 • toClass P = 0`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_grp_nsmul_eq_zero` (+ the `Module (ZMod n) ↥⟨G⟩` instance, `secp256k1Gₙ`)
theorems.claim: The concrete secp256k1 subgroup `⟨G⟩` is a `ZMod n`-module (`secp256k1_grp_nsmul_eq_zero`: every point of `⟨G⟩ = zmultiples secp256k1G` is annihilated by `n`, so `⟨G⟩` carries a `Module (ZMod n)` structure built via `AddCommGroup.zmodModule`. This realises the abstract discrete-log model on a genuine elliptic-curve group — Mathlib provides no automatic `ZMod n`-module on a cyclic subgroup; it must be constructed from the exponent bound, which the weak keystone `addOrderOf secp256k1G = n` supplies. The bridge that lets every abstract protocol theorem instantiate on real `WeierstrassCurve.Affine.Point` values)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_dh_agree`, `secp256k1_schnorr_verify`, `secp256k1_taproot_tweak_verify`, `secp256k1_adaptor_complete
theorems.claim: Deployed signature/agreement protocols are correct on the concrete secp256k1 group (the abstract `Ecdlp.Schnorr` correctness theorems instantiated at the concrete carrier `↥⟨G⟩` with base point `secp256k1Gₙ`, about genuine curve points: Diffie–Hellman agreement `secp256k1_dh_agree`, Schnorr/EdDSA verification `secp256k1_schnorr_verify`, Bitcoin Taproot key-tweak `secp256k1_taproot_tweak_verify` (BIP-341), adaptor-signature completeness `secp256k1_adaptor_complete` (atomic swaps / Lightning PTLCs). Contingent only on `ord(G) = n` + the abstract algebra — the honest content: the real secp256k1 protocols verify on the actual curve subgroup, not only an abstract module)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_threshold_schnorr_aggregate`, `secp256k1_musig_key_aggregate`, `secp256k1_feldman_vss_verify`, `secp256k1_threshold_elgamal_combine`, `secp256k1_schnorr_batch_verify
theorems.claim: *(instantiation, multiparty protocols)* the same concrete-`⟨G⟩` realization for the multi-signer / threshold protocols — aggregate Schnorr / Taproot multisig `secp256k1_threshold_schnorr_aggregate` (MuSig/FROST), MuSig2 key aggregation `secp256k1_musig_key_aggregate`, Feldman VSS share verification `secp256k1_feldman_vss_verify` (DKG), threshold ElGamal partial-decryption combination `secp256k1_threshold_elgamal_combine`, batch Schnorr verification `secp256k1_schnorr_batch_verify

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_grp_card`, `secp256k1_grp_isAddCyclic
theorems.claim: The secp256k1 crypto subgroup `⟨G⟩` is cyclic of order exactly `n` (`secp256k1_grp_card`: `Nat.card ⟨G⟩ = n`, from `Nat.card (zmultiples g) = addOrderOf g` + the weak keystone; `secp256k1_grp_isAddCyclic`: `IsAddCyclic ⟨G⟩`. The group-object-level statement of "⟨G⟩ is a concrete cyclic group of order `n`" — no Hasse/`#E`, only `addOrderOf G = n`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_dlogEquiv` (+ `secp256k1_dlogHom`, `_injective`, `_surjective`, `secp256k1_gen_addOrderOf`)
theorems.claim: The discrete-log map is a group isomorphism `ZMod n ≃+ ⟨G⟩` (`secp256k1_dlogEquiv`: base-point multiplication `c ↦ c·G` is an additive-group isomorphism between the scalar ring `ZMod n` and the concrete secp256k1 subgroup `⟨G⟩` — the exact group-theoretic content of the ECDLP setting: the discrete log `dlog = (c ↦ c·G)⁻` is a well-defined bijection. Its *computational* inversion is the open ECDLP hardness conjecture; this asserts only the unconditional bijection, via injective (`c.val < n = addOrderOf G`) + surjective (`zmultiples`) on the `ZMod n`-module `⟨G⟩`. Built with `AddEquiv.ofBijective`)

theorems.name: WeierstrassCurve.Affine.instFinitePoint
theorems.claim: Affine Weierstrass points are finite over any finite ring — general lemma (Mathlib-gap upstream candidate) (`instFinitePoint`: `Finite W.Point` for every `W : WeierstrassCurve.Affine R` with `[CommRing R] [Finite R]`, via `Point ↪ Option (R × R)`. Weakest hypotheses — no `Field`/`Nontrivial`. Mathlib v4.31.0 has no `Finite`/`Fintype` instance on any `Point` variant (gap confirmed against pinned source); natural upstream home `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/EllipticCurve/Affine/Point.lean`. Subsumes the two per-curve instances below, now one-line `inferInstance` corollaries. Pure-kernel)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.instFiniteSecp256k1Point
theorems.claim: secp256k1 rational-point group is finite (`instFiniteSecp256k1Point`: `Finite secp256k1.toAffine.Point`, now a one-line `inferInstance` corollary of the general `WeierstrassCurve.Affine.instFinitePoint` since `ZMod Secp256k1.p` is a finite commutative ring. Makes Mathlib's `Nat.card E.toAffine.Point` (as used in `EllipticCurve/LFunction.lean`) a real quantity rather than junk `0`. Pure-kernel, no native_decide)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_n_dvd_card_point
theorems.claim: n ∣ #E(𝔽_p)` — base-point order divides the curve cardinality (`secp256k1_n_dvd_card_point`: `Secp256k1.n ∣ Nat.card secp256k1.toAffine.Point`, by Lagrange on `⟨G⟩` (order the proved `n`) + finiteness. The first verified fact about `#E` itself — all prior point-counting stayed inside `⟨G⟩`. Pins `#E ∈ {n, 2n, 3n, …}`; the strong keystone `#E = n` is now proved on top, curve-specifically without Hasse/Schoof (`CurveCardinalityExact.lean`). Compiler-trusted: inherits `Lean.ofReduceBool` from the order keystone `secp256k1_generator_addOrderOf` via `secp256k1_grp_card` — the Lagrange/finiteness step itself adds no `native_decide`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_card_point_eq_n`, `secp256k1_no_nonzero_two_torsion`, `secp256k1_card_point_le
theorems.claim: The exact curve cardinality `#E(𝔽_p) = n` — cofactor 1, without Hasse or Schoof (`secp256k1_card_point_eq_n`: `Nat.card secp256k1.toAffine.Point = Secp256k1.n`). A curve-specific certificate that upgrades `n ∣ #E` to equality using the special structure of `E : Y = X³ + 7` over `p ≡ 1 (mod 3)`: `#E ≤ 2p+1` (`secp256k1_card_point_le`, ≤2 `y` per `x`) and `2p+1 < 3n` force `#E ∈ {n, 2n}`; `2n` (even) is excluded because additive Cauchy would yield a nonzero 2-torsion point, but `E[2] = {O}` (`secp256k1_no_nonzero_two_torsion`: `2•P=0 ⟹ P=0`, since `y=0 ⟹ x³=−7` and `−7` is not a cube in `𝔽_p`, `(−7)^((p−1)/3)≠1`). Removes the last standing assumption behind Units 1–2 (the published order `n` as `#E`). Uses `native_decide` for the 256-bit non-cube leaf and the literal arithmetic (`Lean.ofReduceBool`); numeric facts independently re-checked in `scripts/certs/cardinality_secp256k1.py`, prints `CERT_OK`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_grp_eq_top`, `secp256k1_point_isAddCyclic`, `secp256k1_mem_zmultiples`, `secp256k1_glvHom_eq_zsmul_unconditional
theorems.claim: The whole point group equals `⟨G⟩` — `E(𝔽_p)` is cyclic, `glvPoint = [λ]` unconditional (`secp256k1_grp_eq_top`: `secp256k1Grp = ⊤`, from `#⟨G⟩ = n = #E` via `AddSubgroup.eq_top_of_card_eq`). Upgrades every `⟨G⟩`-only result to the full rational-point group now that `#E = n` is proved: `secp256k1_point_isAddCyclic` (`IsAddCyclic E(𝔽_p)`, an instance), `secp256k1_mem_zmultiples` (every point is a multiple of `G`), and `secp256k1_glvHom_eq_zsmul_unconditional` (the GLV eigenvalue property with the `[IsAddCyclic]` hypothesis now discharged — `glvHom = [k]` with `k+k+1` annihilating every point, no remaining hypotheses). Resolves the `⟨G⟩`-vs-`E(𝔽_p)` scope ambiguity. Pure-kernel on top of the keystone

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_pointGroupEquiv` (+ `secp256k1_point_group_equiv_exists`)
theorems.claim: The full point-group structure theorem: `E(𝔽_p) ≃+ ℤ/n` (`secp256k1_pointGroupEquiv`: base-point multiplication `c ↦ c·G` is an additive-group isomorphism from the scalar ring `ZMod n` onto the entire rational-point group `E(𝔽_p)`, not merely `⟨G⟩`. Lifts the subgroup discrete-log iso `secp256k1_dlogEquiv` off `⟨G⟩` by transporting across cofactor 1 `⟨G⟩ = ⊤` (`AddEquiv.addSubgroupCongr secp256k1_grp_eq_top`) then stripping the `⊤` subtype (`AddSubgroup.topEquiv`). The complete group-theoretic classification of secp256k1's points: cyclic of order `n`, i.e. `≅ ℤ/n`. Unconditional given `#E = n`; computational inversion is the open ECDLP conjecture, the bijection is a theorem. Inherits `Lean.ofReduceBool` through the order keystone)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_point_group_no_proper_subgroup`, `secp256k1_nonzero_point_generates
theorems.claim: secp256k1's point group has no proper nontrivial subgroup — group-level cofactor-1 security (`secp256k1_point_group_no_proper_subgroup`: every `AddSubgroup` of `E(𝔽_p)` is `⊥` or `⊤`, since `#E = n` is prime; plus `secp256k1_nonzero_point_generates`: every `P ≠ 0` has `zmultiples P = ⊤`, and an `IsSimpleAddGroup` instance). The point-group (curve) statement of small-subgroup / invalid-subgroup attack resistance — there is no small-order subgroup a point could be confined to. The geometric counterpart of the scalar-ring `secp256k1_scalar_no_proper_subgroup`, proved the same way with `secp256k1_card_point_eq_n` as the cardinality bridge. Pure-kernel on top of the keystone

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_quintuple_x_eq_Φ₅_div_ΨSq₅`, `quint_x_core
theorems.claim: N7@5 — multiplication-by-5 `x`-coordinate formula: `x(5•P) = Φ₅(x)/ΨSq₅(x)` (`secp256k1_quintuple_x_eq_Φ₅_div_ΨSq₅`: whenever `5•P` is an affine point `(X,Y)`, then `X = (Φ 5)(x)/(ΨSq 5)(x)` — the degree-25 numerator over `preΨ₅`, again with no side conditions: the generic branch adds `3P + 2P` — a chord between two nontrivial multiples, prototyping the addition step of the general N7 engine — through `quint_x_core` (12 `linear_combination` certificates incl. a 94+55-monomial master, designed by cofactor-tracked elimination of the three slopes `s₅, s₃, s₂`, kernel-re-verified); the 2-torsion branch (`5P = P`) and 3-torsion branch (`5P = 2P`, reduced to the `n = 2` formula) hold via `Ψ₂Sq ⊥ preΨ₅` / `Ψ₃ ⊥ preΨ₅` certificates. `Φ 5`/`ΨSq 5` eval forms derived through Mathlib's `Φ_ofNat`/`ΨSq_ofNat`/`preΨ'_even` recurrences (`preΨ₆ = Ψ₃(preΨ₅ − preΨ₄)`). All certificates + 25-point (and independent 40-point, different prime) numeric checks: `scripts/certs/quint_mult_formula_check.py` (`CERT_OK`). N7 ladder now `n = 2, 3, 5`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1Bar_three_torsion_structure`, `secp256k1Bar_three_torsion_ncard`, `secp256k1Bar_torsionBy_three_card
theorems.claim: #E[3](𝔽̄_p) = 9` and `E[3] ≅ (ℤ/3)` — node N13 at `n = 3` (`secp256k1Bar_three_torsion_structure`: the 3-torsion subgroup of secp256k1 over the algebraic closure is additively `ZMod 3 × ZMod 3` — the full 2-dimensional `𝔽₃`-plane. Route: the torsion set is proved EQUAL to the explicit 9-element finset `{O} ∪ {(x, ±y) : Ψ₃(x) = 0}` — membership by the closure bridge, `y ≠ 0` at every `Ψ₃`-root by `Ψ₂Sq ⊥ Ψ₃`, the two `y`-values distinct since `char ≠ 2`, exactly `4` distinct roots by the separability certificates; count `1 + 4·2 = 9` by finset card algebra (`secp256k1Bar_three_torsion_ncard`/`_card`/`secp256k1Bar_torsionBy_three_card`); then the kernel-structure lemma N10(iii) classifies. Composes six previously landed rungs (closure bridge, exact root counts, coprimality certificate, `±y`-pairing, N10(iii), torsionBy vocabulary) — the endpoint `N13@3` of the `ψₙ↔E[n]` critical path at `n = 3`)
theorems.method: Mathlib (finset card algebra, `torsionBy`) + closure bridge + separability + coprimality + N10(iii)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1Bar_five_torsion_structure`, `secp256k1Bar_five_nsmul_eq_zero_iff`, `secp256k1Bar_five_torsion_ncard
theorems.claim: #E[5](𝔽̄_p) = 25` and `E[5] ≅ (ℤ/5)` — N13 at `n = 5` (`secp256k1Bar_five_torsion_structure` + the closure bridge `secp256k1Bar_five_nsmul_eq_zero_iff` (`FiveTorsionBridgeBar.lean`, token-identical port of the `𝔽_p` five-bridge incl. `five_core`): the 5-torsion set equals `{O} ∪ {(x, ±y) : preΨ₅(x) = 0}` — 12 distinct roots (separability certificates) × 2 + O = 25 = 5; `y ≠ 0` via `Ψ₂Sq ⊥ preΨ₅`; N10(iii) classifies. Replication of the E[3] pattern)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1Bar_seven_torsion_structure`, `secp256k1Bar_seven_nsmul_eq_zero_iff`, `secp256k1Bar_seven_torsion_ncard
theorems.claim: #E[7](𝔽̄_p) = 49` and `E[7] ≅ (ℤ/7)` — N13 at `n = 7` (`secp256k1Bar_seven_torsion_structure` + the closure bridge `secp256k1Bar_seven_nsmul_eq_zero_iff` (`SevenTorsionBridgeBar.lean`, token-identical port of `seven_master`/`seven_core`): 24 distinct `preΨ₇` roots × 2 + O = 49 = 7; `y ≠ 0` via the new `Ψ₂Sq ⊥ preΨ₇` certificate; N10(iii) classifies. The N13 instance family is now `n ∈ {3, 5, 7}` — every odd prime with a landed torsion bridge; the general-`n` theorem remains the open program (separability/N10 general core))

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_quadruple_x_eq_Φ₄_div_ΨSq₄`, `quad_x_core
theorems.claim: N7@4 — multiplication-by-4 `x`-coordinate formula: `x(4•P) = Φ₄(x)/ΨSq₄(x)` (`secp256k1_quadruple_x_eq_Φ₄_div_ΨSq₄`: whenever `4•P` is an affine point `(X,Y)`, `X = (Φ 4)(x)/(ΨSq 4)(x)` with the degree-16 monic numerator `Φ₄(x) = x⁶ − 3808x³ + 144256x⁰ + 2985472x⁷ + 38108672x⁴ + 68841472x` and degree-15 denominator `ΨSq₄ = preΨ₄·Ψ₂Sq` — the even rung `n = 4` of the N7 ladder (`n = 2, 3, 4, 5`). The generic branch runs doubling-of-doubling (`4P = 2·(2P)`, two tangents — vs. the odd chords), and its crux is that the outer doubling is the `n = 2` formula applied at `X2 = x(2P)`: `quad_x_core` chains `hInner` (the `n=2` identity `(s₄−2X2)(4X2³+28) = X2⁴−56X2` at `X2`), `hBridge`, `hkey`; both 2-torsion escapes (`2P = O` or `2P` 2-torsion ⟹ `4P = O`) contradict an affine `4P`. Eval forms via Mathlib `Φ_ofNat`/`ΨSq_ofNat`/`preΨ'_even`; `Φ₄`/`ΨSq₄` independently verified against from-scratch division polynomials + 2-prime numeric `x(4P)` check (`scripts/certs/quad_mult_formula_check.py`, `CERT_OK`))

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_quadruple_y`, `quad_y_core`, `secp256k1_four_nsmul_coords_ωfree`, `secp256k1_omega_recurrence_four`, `secp256k1_psi6_evalEval
theorems.claim: N7@4 — multiplication-by-4 `y`-coordinate formula `y(4•P) = ω₄/ψ₄³` + fully closed `carrier_four` leaf (`secp256k1_quadruple_y`: whenever `4•P` is an affine point `(X,Y)`, `Y·ψ₄³ = ω₄(x)` with `ψ₄ = (2x⁶+280x³−784)(2y)` and the degree-24 `ω₄(x) = x⁴+8624x−2875712x⁸−16946944x⁵−1054135040x−35487778816x⁹−229379784704x⁶−701632282624x³−188900999168` — the `y`-companion of the landed `x(4P)=Φ₄/ΨSq₄`, and the `n=4` analogue of `y(2P)=ω₂/(2y)³` / `y(3P)=ω₃/ψ₃³`. The core `quad_y_core` runs doubling-of-doubling (tangents `s₂` at `P`, `s₄` at `2P`) and closes by three CAS-designed `linear_combination` certificates assembled via the exact identity `(2Y2)³·4096y = ψ₄³`. Fed by the freshly-derived even-index brick `secp256k1_psi6_evalEval` (`ψ6 = 2y·(3x⁶+4704x³−131712x⁰−7639296x⁷−12907776x⁴−103262208x)`) and its anchor `secp256k1_omega_recurrence_four` (`ψ₆ψ₃−ψ₂ψ₅ = 4y·ω₄`), it reshapes into the joint ω-free carrier leaf `secp256k1_four_nsmul_coords_ωfree`, which closes `carrier_four` (both conjuncts) in the uniform induction stem — the `n=4` (even) base rung of `n7_uniform_secp256k1_x`. `ω₄`/`ψ₆` CAS-validated (on-curve `(A,B)` representation) + 10-prime numeric `Y(4P)·ψ₄³=ω₄` check. Honest scope: fixed `n=4`; the uniform induction over all `n` stays open. No `native_decide`, no new axioms)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1_cubic_separable`, `secp256k1_cubic_isCoprime_derivative`, `secp256k1_cubic_roots_card_bar`, `secp256k1_cubic_roots_nodup_bar
theorems.claim: X³ + 7` is separable — exactly 3 distinct roots over `𝔽̄_p` (`secp256k1_cubic_separable` + `secp256k1_cubic_roots_card_bar` = 3 / `_nodup_bar`: the 2-torsion cubic `X³+7` (whose roots are the `x`-coordinates of the 2-torsion, `Ψ₂Sq = 4(X³+7)`) is coprime to its derivative `3X` by a trivial Bézout certificate `u₀·(X³+7) + (v₀X)·(3X) = 1` (`u₀ = 7⁻`, `v₀ = −21⁻`; `p ∤ 21`), so it is separable and splits with `natDegree = 3` distinct roots over the closure — the `n = 2` analogue of the `secp256k1_Ψ₃_separable` family, the counting brick of E[2]. Constants cert-verified `scripts/certs/cubic_separable_cert.py` (`CERT_OK`))

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Curve.secp256k1Bar_two_torsion_structure`, `secp256k1Bar_two_nsmul_eq_zero_iff`, `secp256k1Bar_two_torsion_ncard
theorems.claim: #E[2](𝔽̄_p) = 4` and `E[2] ≅ (ℤ/2)` — N13 at `n = 2`, completing the family to `n ∈ {2,3,5,7}` (`secp256k1Bar_two_torsion_structure` + the closure characterization `secp256k1Bar_two_nsmul_eq_zero_iff`: `2•P = O ⟺ y = 0`. Structurally the `n = 2` N13 instance — no multiplication formula: `2•P = O ⟺ P = −P ⟺ y = negY = −y ⟺ y = 0` (`char ≠ 2`), and `y = 0` on the curve forces `x³+7 = 0`. So `E[2] = {O} ∪ {(x,0) : X³+7-root}`, an explicit 4-element finset (no `±y` split — `y = 0` is forced), `1 + 3 = 4 = 2`; N10(iii) at prime 2 classifies. The even companion of the odd family)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.evalFracAt_well_defined`, `evalFracAt_mul`, `evalAt_unit_ne_zero`, `evalRatAt_eq_evalFracAt`, `secp256k1_miller_eval_scaling
theorems.claim: W3 evaluation layer completed at the fraction level — `evalFracAt` + the Miller-representative nonvanishing (`evalFracAt h a b := evalAt h a / evalAt h b` with the representation-independence core `evalFracAt_well_defined` (cross-multiplication `a₁b₂ = a₂b₁` + nonvanishing denominators ⟹ equal values — transported through the ring hom `evalAt`, no domain instance needed), Miller-loop multiplicativity `evalFracAt_mul`, unit facts (`evalAt_unit_ne_zero`: coordinate-ring units never vanish at rational points; `evalFracAt_unit_mul`), and the bridge to the localization layer `evalRatAt_eq_evalFracAt` (an `a/b` presentation of a rational function regular at `P` evaluates identically under the residue map). Plus the secp256k1 Miller-layer theorem `secp256k1_miller_eval_scaling`: the W2 Miller-function representative ambiguity is a globally nonvanishing unit factor at every rational point — the well-definedness seed for the eventual `eₙ`. Honest scope: extracting an `a/b` presentation from an abstract `FunctionField` element is the documented next rung; curve-agnostic sections PR-able)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.functionField_exists_num_den`, `functionField_num_den_cross`, `evalFracAt_num_den_well_defined`, `secp256k1_miller_function_num_den
theorems.claim: Function-field `a/b` extraction — every rational function is a quotient of regular functions (`functionField_exists_num_den`: every `f` in the curve's function field (`FractionRing F[E]`, definitionally) equals `algebraMap a / algebraMap b` with `b ≠ 0` — via `IsLocalization.surj` on the nonZeroDivisors localization; with the division-free form (`functionField_exists_mul_num_den`), cross-multiplication faithfulness of two presentations (`functionField_num_den_cross`), nonzero-numerator (`functionField_num_ne_zero`), the weld to `evalFracAt` (`evalFracAt_num_den_well_defined`: presentations regular at `P` evaluate equally), and the Miller instantiation `secp256k1_miller_function_num_den` (the W2 generator `f_P` = an explicit quotient `↑a/↑b`, and the fractional ideal is generated by that quotient). Honest scope: the extraction controls `b ≠ 0` but not *where* `b` vanishes — choosing a presentation with `b(P) ≠ 0` for `f` regular at `P` is the documented next rung, then `f_P(D_Q)` and W4)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.RegularAt`, `evalReg`, `evalRatAt_eq_evalReg` (+ 14 supporting)
theorems.claim: W3 closed at the function-field level — `RegularAt` and evaluation of abstract elements (`FunctionFieldRegular.lean`: the `RegularAt` predicate (`∃ a b, evalAt b ≠ 0 ∧ f = a/b`), the unique value `evalReg` with its defining lemmas, and the two-way bridge to the localization: an element of `Localization.AtPrime (XYIdeal P)` maps to a `RegularAt` function (via `IsLocalization.surj` + `evalAt_ker` rewriting `b ∉ XYIdeal ⟺ evalAt b ≠ 0`), and conversely a regular presentation determines a localization element (`IsLocalization.mk'`), with the payoff weld `evalRatAt = evalReg` on the overlap. With this, every abstract function-field element regular at `P` has a well-defined value at `P` — the W3 evaluation half is closed end-to-end (`evalAt → evalRatAt → evalFracAt → a/b extraction → RegularAt`); next: `f_P(D_Q)` divisor evaluation and W4 reciprocity)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.divEval_mul`, `evalReg_mul
theorems.claim: Weil ladder W3e-1 — divisor evaluation `f((Q)−(O))` is multiplicative in the function (`divEval_mul`: for `f, g` regular at both `Q` and `O`, `divEval (f·g) = divEval f · divEval g`, where `divEval f := evalReg f Q / evalReg f O` is the value of `f` at the degree-0 divisor `(Q)−(O)`; via the pointwise multiplicativity `evalReg_mul` — `evalReg (f·g) = evalReg f · evalReg g` at a point, from `evalReg_eq` + `evalFracAt_mul`. The homomorphism property that makes `f_P(D_Q)` a map `E → μₙ` in the Weil pairing; first evaluation-half rung of `notes/WEIL_LADDER.md`)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.divEval_smul_unit`, `divEval_smul_unit_eq`, `evalReg_smul_unit
theorems.claim: Weil ladder W3e-2 — how the Miller representative enters `divEval`, and conditional representative-independence (`divEval_smul_unit`: for `g = u·f` with `u` a unit of `F[E]` and `f, g` regular at `Q, O`, `divEval g = (u(Q)/u(O)) · divEval f` — the entire dependence of `divEval` on the choice of Miller function is the single ratio `u(Q)/u(O)`, from the pointwise `evalReg_smul_unit` = `evalReg (u·f) = u(P)·evalReg f` via `evalFracAt_unit_mul`; and `divEval_smul_unit_eq`: if additionally `u(Q) = u(O)` the ratio is `1`, so `divEval g = divEval f`. secp256k1 forms `secp256k1_divEval_miller_rep_scaling`/`_indep` apply this to the `secp256k1_miller_function_unique` ambiguity. Honest scope: unconditional independence needs `u(Q)=u(O)`, i.e. that units of `F[E]` are constants — absent from Mathlib v4.31, carried as an explicit hypothesis, not proved)

theorems.name: Ecdlp.Weil.secp256k1_miller_jointly_regular`, `divEval_one
theorems.claim: Weil ladder W3e-3 — the raw pairing value's domain (support-disjointness bridge) + the `divEval` unit law (`secp256k1_miller_jointly_regular`: a Miller function `f` for `(P,n)` has a fixed presentation `a/b`, and at any two points `A, B` where the single denominator `b` does not vanish — i.e. `A, B` off `supp(div f)` — `f` is regular at both, the joint-regularity hypothesis `divEval hA hB` consumes, from one geometric condition; and `divEval_one`/`evalReg_one`: `divEval 1 ((A)−(B)) = 1`, which with `divEval_mul` makes `divEval` a monoid hom on the functions regular at both points. Closes the reachable W3-evaluation scaffolding. Honest scope: this packages the raw value's domain + algebra; it does not assemble `eₙ(P,Q)` — that needs Weil reciprocity, the frozen W4 no-go, since the base point `O ∈ supp(div f_P)`)

blockers.title: No uniform Point-to-division-polynomial multiplication map
blockers.description: Mathlib exposes the polynomial identities but not the theorem connecting [n]P to the phi/psi coordinate formulas for all n.
blockers.resume_condition: An upstream multiplication-coordinate theorem lands, or a reviewed in-repo induction closes the torsion bridge.

blockers.title: Four N7 algebra walls need large checked elimination certificates
blockers.description: The statements are machine-elaborated and numerically/symbolically validated, but checked cofactors for the degree-heavy identities are not yet authored.
blockers.resume_condition: A reproducible certificate generator emits Lean-checkable cofactors for all four algebra walls.

blockers.title: Uniform separability of multiplication-by-n is not available
blockers.description: Small n is closed, while the general prime-to-characteristic counting and differential argument remains absent.
blockers.resume_condition: A checked [n]*omega=n omega or equivalent general separability theorem is available.

blockers.title: Weil reciprocity and divisor-degree machinery are missing
blockers.description: The reachable W1-W3 evaluation substrate is closed; W4 reciprocity is required before the pairing can be assembled.
blockers.resume_condition: Mathlib or this repository gains the needed divisor/tame-symbol reciprocity layer.

blockers.title: P-256 exact cardinality needs a general point-counting/Hasse route
blockers.description: Only n divides the P-256 group cardinality is proved; the secp256k1 j=0 certificate does not transfer.
blockers.resume_condition: A reviewed Hasse/Schoof or curve-specific exact-cardinality proof becomes available.

critical_nodes.title: Canonical counts, registries, generated views, and active queue
Exact rational-point cardinality and full cyclic group
Abstract protocol algebra and concrete curve instantiation
GLV automorphism on the full rational point group
Division-polynomial, EDS, degree, and coprimality substrate
Closed E[n] structure for n in {2,3,5,7} and fixed multiplication rungs through 5
Weil divisor, Miller function, and reachable evaluation layer
Uniform separability and geometric n-torsion count
General E[n] structure over the algebraic closure
Weil reciprocity and the bilinear non-degenerate pairing

target_problem.output: Recover k modulo ord(P). The primary target fixes P to a generator of the prime-order secp256k1 group, so ord(P) = n.

threat_models.definition: One arbitrary public pair (P,Q) on secp256k1, no leakage, no auxiliary powers, no useful interval promise, and no attacker-chosen invalid inputs.
The group is available only through generic encoding and group-operation oracles.
The attacker receives extra structure such as many targets, reusable precomputation, an interval promise, or auxiliary powers of the secret.
Nonce bias, side channels, faults, invalid-curve acceptance, or protocol misuse leaks information beyond a plain ECDLP instance.
A sufficiently capable error-corrected quantum computer can execute an ECDLP algorithm and its reversible elliptic-curve arithmetic.

allowed_work: Ingest new primary literature or a concrete candidate proposal and evaluate it against the common gate.
Maintain canonical registries, generated views, and independent validation infrastructure.
Close narrow formal scope statements that materially change a route disposition.
forbidden_work: Start or resume a cryptanalytic experiment without a new explicit route-selection decision and matching task/hypothesis promotion.
Build a missing Mathlib foundation merely because it is absent or mathematically interesting.
Treat a constant-factor result, structural identity, or toy measurement as a subgeneric attack.
Delete or rewrite provenance-bearing assets without an inventory, reference scan, and documented restore path.
phase_policy.promotion_rule: A future dated decision may promote at most one parked route only after new evidence satisfies the acceptance gate at proposal level and tasks/NEXT.md plus experiments/HYPOTHESES.yaml explicitly authorize the same work.
phase_policy.merge_rule: The owner delegated merge authority on 2026-07-22. Merge only from a clean branch after required CI is green, the diff has a documented scope and rollback path, and no unresolved blocking finding is known.

route_selection.gate_result: No audited route currently satisfies every proposal-level requirement for a new experiment against the primary plain single-target secp256k1 objective.
rationale: The generic lower bound and generic algorithms are guardrails or baselines, not non-generic attack mechanisms.
GLV supplies a verified constant-factor structure; Pohlig-Hellman, low-degree pairing transfer, anomalous lifting, and extension-field descent fail target-specific applicability screens.
The open prime-field algebraic, GLV-Semaev, Petit-style, EDS/division-polynomial, and transfer directions do not yet provide an exact nonredundant mechanism plus a justified subgeneric cost bridge.
Multi-target, interval, leakage, implementation, and quantum routes change the input or computational threat model and therefore cannot be promoted as a classical plain-input result.
Building conditional Mathlib foundations now would increase theorem volume without a demonstrated reduction in uncertainty about the exact target.
reconsideration_triggers: A primary source or reproducible proposal identifies an exact non-generic mechanism for the plain target and supplies a theoretical full-cost argument.
New verified target structure invalidates an existing applicability or stop-condition decision.
A deliberately approved project objective changes the threat model to conditioned inputs, implementation leakage, or fault-tolerant quantum computation.
operational_effects: No conditional formal foundation is promoted.
Candidate intake and evidence maintenance continue through the neutral evaluation contract.
Independent adversarial review remains useful when another reviewer is available, but is not a merge prerequisite.

routes.title: Generic-group lower bound
assumptions: Generic encodings reveal no exploitable representation structure.
Complexity is measured in oracle queries with bounded success probability.
routes.known_cost: Omega(sqrt(n)) generic queries, under the exact formal model and success assumptions.
routes.current_evidence: The collision-counting core and square-root consequence are Lean-verified.
routes.success_gate: Any classical plain-input route must identify and justify the non-generic information that escapes this model.
routes.stop_condition: Reject proposals that merely relabel generic operations or hide work in preprocessing.
routes.next_action: Retain as a mandatory comparison and scope guard; no new experiment.
routes.anti_overclaim: This is not an unconditional lower bound for algorithms exploiting field representation, endomorphisms, leakage, auxiliary inputs, or quantum computation.

routes.title: Baby-step giant-step and Pollard rho
assumptions: Ord(P) is known.
No special target structure is required.
routes.known_cost: Theta(sqrt(n)) group work; BSGS uses Theta(sqrt(n)) memory while rho can use negligible memory with parallel variants.
routes.current_evidence: Core decomposition, step bound, and collision/periodicity statements are Lean-verified; implementation constants are not a secp256k1 break.
routes.success_gate: Every candidate route must beat the correct time-memory-precomputation baseline under the same input model.
routes.stop_condition: Do not present hardware scaling or parallel rho as an asymptotic improvement.
routes.next_action: Use as the benchmark comparator in the evaluation harness.
routes.anti_overclaim: A faster implementation, distributed search, or smaller constant does not change the square-root exponent.

routes.title: GLV endomorphism acceleration
assumptions: Use the secp256k1 order-3 endomorphism and its eigenvalue on the generator subgroup.
routes.known_cost: Known use is a constant-factor acceleration or search-space symmetry; no justified o(sqrt(n)) plain-input algorithm follows.
routes.current_evidence: The rational point action, eigenvalue scope on multiples of G, and orbit facts are Lean-verified.
routes.success_gate: Promotion requires a mechanism that changes relation-generation or search complexity, not only orbit size.
routes.stop_condition: Close any proposal whose only gain is a finite orbit quotient or scalar decomposition.
routes.next_action: Keep as target structure available to a selected route; do not treat it as a standalone attack.
routes.anti_overclaim: The proved GLV identities and a factor-of-three orbit reduction are structural or constant-factor facts, not a subgeneric ECDLP result.

routes.title: Pohlig-Hellman subgroup reduction
routes.applicability: the algorithm applies, but secp256k1 has no smaller prime-order factors to exploit
assumptions: A useful factorization of ord(P) into smaller factors.
routes.known_cost: Dominated by the largest prime factor of ord(P); for secp256k1 that factor is n itself.
routes.current_evidence: n is Lean-verified prime and the reduction/reconstruction algebra is formalized.
routes.success_gate: A proposal must exhibit an actual nontrivial subgroup decomposition of the target instance.
routes.stop_condition: Stop immediately for the standard generator subgroup because n is prime.
routes.next_action: Retain as a machine-checked elimination certificate.
routes.anti_overclaim: Prime order rules out this decomposition route; it does not by itself prove hardness against non-generic attacks.

routes.title: MOV, Frey-Ruck, and Tate/Weil pairing transfer
routes.applicability: no useful low-embedding-degree transfer is available for secp256k1
assumptions: The n-torsion embeds into a finite-field multiplicative group of tractable extension degree.
The relevant pairing is non-degenerate on available points.
routes.known_cost: Transfer is only useful when finite-field DLP in F_(p^k) is easier at the target's embedding degree k.
routes.current_evidence: Lean proves the secp256k1 embedding degree is greater than 100, which is a strong practical exclusion of low-degree MOV-style transfers.
routes.success_gate: A proposal must state the exact pairing, available torsion points, extension degree, and finite-field DLP cost.
routes.stop_condition: Reject low-degree-transfer claims inconsistent with the verified embedding-degree bound.
routes.next_action: Keep the target-specific exclusion; defer a full pairing library unless a new route needs it.
routes.anti_overclaim: The proved bound excludes low-degree practical transfer in the audited model; it is not a theorem that every conceivable pairing-based reduction is impossible.

routes.title: Smart, Satoh-Araki, and Semaev anomalous-curve lifting
routes.applicability: inapplicable because secp256k1 is not anomalous
assumptions: #E(F_p) = p, equivalently trace one in the relevant setting.
routes.known_cost: Polynomial-time for the anomalous special case; the prerequisite fails here.
routes.current_evidence: Lean proves the anomalous iff trace-one scope and that secp256k1 is ordinary and non-anomalous.
routes.success_gate: A p-adic proposal must identify a different valid degeneracy and prove it holds for secp256k1.
routes.next_action: Retain the formal exclusion; do not build an elliptic formal-group stack for this target now.
routes.anti_overclaim: Ruling out the anomalous-curve attack does not rule out unrelated lifting or representation-specific algorithms.

routes.title: Weil descent and GHS-style transfer
routes.applicability: the standard extension-field descent mechanism has no nontrivial degree to exploit over the prime field F_p
assumptions: The curve is defined over an extension field with exploitable descent geometry.
routes.known_cost: Useful only for selected extension-field settings; degree one gives no descent gain.
routes.current_evidence: Target-field structure rules out the standard GHS setting; no dedicated Lean theorem is claimed.
routes.success_gate: A proposal must provide a concrete alternative descent object over F_p and a lower resulting DLP cost.
routes.stop_condition: Reject arguments that silently replace the prime-field target with an extension-field family.
routes.next_action: Document as a target-applicability exclusion; no formal stack now.
routes.anti_overclaim: This excludes the standard extension-field descent setup, not every possible algebraic-geometric reformulation over a prime field.

routes.title: Prime-field index calculus
routes.applicability: conceptually direct, with no validated subgeneric secp256k1 algorithm in the audited evidence
assumptions: Efficient factor-base relation generation over a large prime field.
A tractable polynomial-system solving regime.
Linear algebra and descent costs that preserve any relation-generation gain.
routes.known_cost: No accepted plain-prime-field asymptotic improvement is established here; known toy systems encounter relation-yield, degree, and memory barriers.
routes.current_evidence: S3/S4 foundations and limited toy measurements exist, but they do not establish a degree-of-regularity law or subgeneric scaling.
routes.success_gate: Provide an exact relation system, independent EC validation, a justified solving-cost model, and multi-size evidence consistent with o(sqrt(n)).
routes.stop_condition: Park when gains rely only on factor-base constants, unvalidated Macaulay proxies, tiny fields, or hidden exhaustive enumeration.
routes.next_action: Preserve evidence and prerequisites; wait for explicit route selection before any new experiment.
routes.anti_overclaim: Solving selected toy systems or proving Semaev identities does not establish a practical or asymptotic ECDLP improvement.

routes.title: GLV-symmetrized Semaev systems
routes.applicability: direct to the j=0 target structure, but only constant-factor evidence exists
assumptions: Invariant coordinates reduce relation-generation complexity or solving degree, not only factor-base storage.
The quotient retains enough information to recover valid relations.
routes.known_cost: Measured finite-orbit reductions are constant-factor; the relevant higher-degree invariant-system cost is unresolved.
routes.current_evidence: Lean verifies the GLV and Semaev structural ingredients. Existing experiments close only narrow lookup and toy-presentation windows.
routes.success_gate: Exhibit a nonredundant invariant S_m system and a theoretical reason for a lower asymptotic solving exponent, then validate it independently.
routes.stop_condition: Stop if the gain is bounded by orbit size or disappears after complete relation-generation and recovery costs are counted.
routes.next_action: Keep HYP_GLV_SEMAEV_001 parked until route selection explicitly promotes it.
routes.anti_overclaim: The mathematical question remains open while execution is parked; prior toy results are partial negative evidence, not an active attack.

routes.title: Petit-style composed rational maps
routes.applicability: unresolved because the repository has not implemented a faithful construction
assumptions: A correctly specified low-degree rational map yields a useful factor-base or relation system.
Composition, solving, and recovery costs improve the full attack.
routes.known_cost: No reliable project estimate; the existing six-variable toy presentation is explicitly non-faithful and descriptive.
routes.current_evidence: The current experiment is useful only as a negative control for one toy presentation.
routes.success_gate: First reproduce the exact construction from a verified primary source, then compare it under the common evaluation contract.
routes.stop_condition: Reject low-bit filters, redundant auxiliary variables, or presentation-only degree changes as substitutes for the cited construction.
routes.next_action: Park until the source specification and route-selection gate justify implementation.
routes.anti_overclaim: The existing P4 experiment is not a faithful Petit implementation and cannot support a claim about the original method.

routes.title: Elliptic divisibility sequences and division-polynomial re-encodings
routes.applicability: structurally direct, with no known independent information source or subgeneric inversion method
assumptions: A sequence or polynomial observable can be inverted more cheaply than scalar multiplication or exhaustive apparition search.
routes.known_cost: Known recurrences evaluate W_m for a supplied m; they do not cheaply find the unknown first zero or discrete logarithm.
routes.current_evidence: Lean formalizes substantial division-polynomial and normEDS structure. Toy measurements confirm apparition/torsion equivalence and no advantage.
routes.success_gate: Identify an observable computable from (P,Q) whose inversion provably avoids equivalent ECDLP work.
routes.stop_condition: Stop when the construction only re-encodes scalar multiplication or requires Theta(ord(P)) search.
routes.next_action: Keep HYP_WARD_EDS_001 parked; finish no additional point-division bridge unless a selected route needs it.
routes.anti_overclaim: A fast recurrence for a known index and a verified torsion identity do not solve for an unknown scalar.

routes.title: Isogeny, endomorphism-ring, and Frobenius transfer
routes.applicability: no concrete efficiently computable transfer to a weaker target is identified
assumptions: An efficiently computable map reaches a curve or representation where DLP is materially easier.
Kernel and degree do not erase the gain.
routes.known_cost: Unspecified without a concrete target and map; special endomorphisms currently support constant-factor GLV acceleration.
routes.current_evidence: The repository has target endomorphism facts but no general isogeny stack or candidate weak codomain.
routes.success_gate: Provide an explicit map, target, DLP advantage, and end-to-end cost.
routes.stop_condition: Do not formalize a general stack in advance of a concrete route.
routes.next_action: Monitor literature and reuse GLV facts; no build now.
routes.anti_overclaim: Complex multiplication and a nontrivial endomorphism do not imply a transfer to an easier DLP.

routes.title: Multi-target and reusable-precomputation tradeoffs
routes.applicability: not applicable to the primary one-target/no-precomputation objective unless costs are explicitly amortized
assumptions: Many targets share a group or base point.
Precomputation storage and setup are available and counted separately.
routes.known_cost: Can change amortized constants and time-memory tradeoffs; it does not by itself solve an arbitrary single target below the proper total-cost baseline.
routes.current_evidence: Covered in the attack registry as a separate conditioned model.
routes.success_gate: State target count, online/offline split, memory, reuse horizon, and total amortized cost.
routes.stop_condition: Reject comparisons that omit precomputation or compare an amortized online phase to a full single-target baseline.
routes.next_action: Represent in the evaluation contract; no experiment in this phase.
routes.anti_overclaim: An improved online phase after large reusable work is not a plain single-target break.

routes.title: Interval DLP, partial-key knowledge, and auxiliary-power algorithms
routes.applicability: not applicable to an arbitrary full-range scalar without the promised interval or auxiliary data
assumptions: k lies in a known small interval, or the attacker receives additional algebraic powers or relations involving k.
routes.known_cost: May reduce work relative to the conditioned search space; it does not alter the unconditioned n-sized instance.
routes.current_evidence: The current target contract supplies neither an interval promise nor auxiliary powers.
routes.success_gate: Declare exactly how the auxiliary information is obtained and include its acquisition cost.
routes.stop_condition: Reject any route that assumes the secret information it is meant to recover.
routes.next_action: Keep as a scope category for future protocol or leakage analyses.
routes.anti_overclaim: Conditioned algorithms solve a different input problem unless the extra information is independently available.

routes.title: Hidden-number and lattice attacks on biased or reused ECDSA nonces
routes.applicability: important for key security but not a plain ECDLP algorithm
assumptions: Signatures leak nonce bits, reuse nonces, or have a statistically exploitable bias.
routes.known_cost: Depends on leakage quality, sample count, and lattice dimension; a valid instance can recover a signing key without solving arbitrary ECDLP.
routes.current_evidence: The repository catalogs the attack family but lacks an LLL/BKZ and HNP formal stack.
routes.success_gate: Keep leakage acquisition, statistical model, lattice reduction, and key verification explicit.
routes.stop_condition: Do not classify nonce failures as a mathematical break of secp256k1 ECDLP.
routes.next_action: Preserve as a separate security track; no lattice foundation build for the primary objective now.
routes.anti_overclaim: Recovering keys from bad nonces demonstrates an implementation or protocol failure, not a generic ECDLP solver.

routes.title: Invalid-curve, twist, fault, and side-channel routes
routes.applicability: relevant only when an implementation exposes attacker-controlled inputs or physical leakage
assumptions: Missing point validation, unsafe scalar multiplication, fault injection, timing, power, cache, or electromagnetic leakage.
routes.known_cost: Implementation-dependent and often far below generic DLP when the vulnerability exists.
routes.current_evidence: Protocol algebra is formalized at an abstract level; implementation leakage is outside that proof model.
routes.success_gate: Specify the concrete implementation, leakage channel, validation behavior, and key-recovery verification.
routes.stop_condition: Keep mathematical protocol soundness separate from implementation hardening claims.
routes.next_action: Record scope; do not expand the Lean substrate until a concrete implementation-verification goal exists.
routes.anti_overclaim: A vulnerable implementation can expose a key even though the underlying plain ECDLP remains unchanged.

routes.title: Fault-tolerant quantum ECDLP
routes.applicability: algorithmically direct given sufficiently capable fault-tolerant quantum hardware
assumptions: Large-scale error-corrected quantum computation.
A reversible elliptic-curve implementation with the quoted logical resources.
Physical error correction overhead is separately accounted for.
routes.known_cost: Polynomial-time quantum algorithm. The 2026 audited preprint reports 835 logical qubits and 2^30.63 Toffoli gates for secp256k1; physical resources and availability are not established by that estimate.
routes.current_evidence: Shor gives the algorithmic route; Luo et al. provide the current project-tracked logical-resource estimate.
routes.success_gate: Distinguish algorithm, logical circuit, error-correction overhead, physical hardware, runtime, and availability.
routes.stop_condition: Do not turn a logical-resource estimate into a claim that present hardware can break secp256k1.
routes.next_action: Update the registry estimate and monitor primary literature; do not build a quantum Lean stack now.
routes.anti_overclaim: This is a separate computational threat model and a future resource risk, not a classical secp256k1 break or evidence of current operational capability.

foundations.title: Threat-model, applicability, and evidence contract
foundations.mathlib_status: Project control-plane work; no upstream mathematical library is required.
foundations.deliverable: Canonical route schema, structural checker, generated view, and task/hypothesis authorization checks.

foundations.title: Independent benchmark oracle and reproducibility manifest
foundations.mathlib_status: Non-kernel experimental infrastructure; Lean is not the execution engine.
foundations.deliverable: A candidate-neutral schema for inputs, outputs, cost counters, provenance hashes, and independent validation. It does not authorize a hypothesis run.
foundations.resume_condition: Build after the decision substrate checker is green.

foundations.title: Formal oracle and resource cost model
foundations.mathlib_status: The audit found no general project-ready oracle-query or algorithmic resource framework covering these comparisons.
foundations.deliverable: A compositional model that separates online time, offline work, memory, parallelism, and success probability.
foundations.resume_condition: A selected route has a concrete cost theorem whose ambiguity cannot be controlled by the non-kernel evaluation schema.

foundations.title: General Semaev elimination and polynomial-system complexity stack
foundations.mathlib_status: Mathlib has multivariate polynomials and division/reduction infrastructure, but the audit found no Semaev family, Buchberger/F4 solver, degree-of-regularity theory, or Macaulay complexity stack.
foundations.deliverable: Only the minimal theorem chain required by a selected exact relation system: semantics, elimination soundness, recovery, and a justified cost bridge.
foundations.resume_condition: One of the dependent routes is selected with an exact nonredundant system and a plausible complexity mechanism.

foundations.title: Uniform point scalar-multiplication to division-polynomial bridge
foundations.mathlib_status: Mathlib provides division polynomials but the audit found no general bridge identifying point nsmul coordinates with psi/phi/omega evaluations.
foundations.deliverable: A general bridge only if an EDS or division-polynomial candidate needs it for a decision-relevant theorem.
foundations.resume_condition: The EDS/division-polynomial route identifies an observable that is not merely a scalar-multiplication re-encoding.

foundations.title: Uniform separability and general n-torsion structure
foundations.mathlib_status: Project-specific low-n results exist; the uniform theorem chain remains blocked in repo/FORMAL_SUBSTRATE.json.
foundations.deliverable: General theorems only when they discharge a selected route's applicability or correctness gate.
foundations.resume_condition: A selected route needs uniform n rather than the already closed concrete small-prime family.

foundations.title: Weil reciprocity and pairing construction
foundations.mathlib_status: The audit found no project-ready Weil/Tate pairing, Miller-function, or Weil-reciprocity stack in the pinned or observed Mathlib source tree.
foundations.deliverable: A full nondegenerate pairing theorem only if a route needs more than the existing target-specific embedding-degree exclusion.
foundations.resume_condition: A concrete pairing route survives the verified embedding-degree applicability screen.

foundations.title: p-adic elliptic formal-group and lifting stack
foundations.mathlib_status: The audit found no elliptic formal-group attack stack in current project dependencies.
foundations.deliverable: None for the current target; retain the verified non-anomalous exclusion.
foundations.resume_condition: A new lifting method with prerequisites actually satisfied by secp256k1 is identified.

foundations.title: Lattice reduction and hidden-number problem stack
foundations.mathlib_status: The audit found no project-ready LLL/BKZ, CVP/SVP, or HNP theorem stack.
foundations.deliverable: A separate implementation-security substrate, not part of the primary plain-ECDLP phase.
foundations.resume_condition: The project explicitly opens an implementation-leakage track with a concrete signer model.

foundations.title: Fault-tolerant quantum circuit and resource model
foundations.mathlib_status: The audit found no quantum-circuit or qubit resource framework in Mathlib suitable for this estimate.
foundations.deliverable: A literature monitor with explicit logical-versus-physical resource separation.
foundations.resume_condition: A project goal changes from classical ECDLP research to formal quantum resource verification.

foundations.title: Elliptic isogeny and endomorphism-ring stack
foundations.mathlib_status: The source-tree audit found no elliptic isogeny module in the pinned or observed Mathlib snapshot.
foundations.deliverable: No general library now; add only the map and facts required by a concrete selected transfer.
foundations.resume_condition: An explicit efficiently computable transfer to a demonstrably weaker DLP target is proposed.

attack_registry_index.family: Generic-group model (Shoup / Nechaev black-box Omega(sqrt n) lower bound)
Baby-step giant-step + Pollard rho / kangaroo (generic square-root collision search)
GLV / CM endomorphism speedup (Pollard-rho automorphism ~sqrt(3); GLV scalar decomposition)
Pohlig-Hellman (prime-factor decomposition of the group order)
Pairing transfer: MOV / Frey-Ruck (Weil) + Tate-Lichtenbaum (DEDUP of TRANSFER-MOV-FR and TRANSFER-TATE)
p-adic / lifting: anomalous Smart-SSSA (Semaev-Satoh-Araki) + xedni calculus (DEDUP of TRANSFER-ANOMALOUS-SSSA and IC-5-padic-lifting)
generic time/memory/data trade-off (batch DLP, DLP-with-preprocessing, Cheon auxiliary-input)
side-channel / implementation: Hidden Number Problem -> lattice reduction (LLL/BKZ, CVP) / Bleichenbacher FFT

corpus.note: 486 atomic claims. formalizable + formalizable_hard are the candidate frontier; informal_only / scope_meta are out of scope by nature.
